function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
As of March 31, 2026, 117M of 550M MAUs used Grok AI features and approximately 350M daily posts were generated. Using these figures and the filing’s metric caveats, what can and can an analyst not conclude regarding Grok’s distribution advantage through X?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about Grok AI's distribution advantage through X (formerly Twitter), using specific figures from the SpaceX S-1 filing: 117M of 550M MAUs used Grok AI features, and approximately 350M daily posts were generated as of March 31, 2026. They want to know what an analyst can and cannot conclude based on these figures and the filing's metric caveats.

Let me first parse the SpaceX S-1 filing to find the relevant sections about Grok, X, MAUs, and the metric caveats.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Let me search for information about Grok AI, MAUs, X platform metrics, and the caveats around those metrics.

arguments.prompt: What are the Grok AI MAU figures, X platform MAU figures, daily posts, and distribution advantage metrics in {{spacex_s1}}?
What are the metric caveats, limitations, and definitions for MAUs, daily posts, and user engagement metrics in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided prospectus summary chunks, here are the requested user metrics, distribution advantages, and the strategic vision for orbital AI data centers:

### 1. Key User & Engagement Metrics (as of March 31, 2026)
*   **Grok AI MAUs:** **~117 million** monthly active users specifically utilized Grok’s AI features.
*   **X Platform / Combined MAUs:** The prospectus reports a **combined, deduplicated MAU figure of ~550 million** across the integrated X and Grok platforms (up from ~520 million in December 2025). *Note: The text explicitly states that their MAU methodology identifies and accounts for users who access both platforms via sign-in traffic so they are not double-counted. A standalone X-only MAU figure is not isolated.*
*   **Supported Accounts:** Over **1.3 billion** supported accounts were active in the trailing twelve months.
*   **Daily Posts:** **~350 million** daily posts. The prospectus defines this metric broadly as the aggregate volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes, and media shared on X, *plus* the real-time interactions, analysis, and generative capabilities provided by Grok (including AI-generated posts).

### 2. Distribution Advantage & Competitive Edge
Grok’s primary competitive differentiator is its deep, native integration with X, which serves as a "foundational distribution and data engine." The specific advantages include:
*   **Real-Time Data Moat:** Grok has proprietary access to X's real-time information stream (~350 million daily posts). This provides "freshness, relevance, and contextual awareness," grounding Grok's "truth-seeking" outputs in up-to-date global events and diverse human discourse rather than relying solely on static training data.
*   **Front-and-Center UI Real Estate:** Grok is natively integrated into the X interface via a prominent icon, allowing users to seamlessly access conversational AI, Grok-assisted post creation, enhanced search, and trending analysis without leaving the app.
*   **Subscription Funnel (X Premium):** X’s subscription tiers (Basic, Premium, and Premium+) act as a built-in distribution and monetization vehicle, offering users expanded features, ad-reduced experiences, and priority Grok interactions.
*   **Developer & API Ecosystem:** X offers developers access to a continuous, high-volume, real-time stream of sentiment and trend data via the X Developer Platform and APIs, further embedding the X/Grok ecosystem into third-party enterprise workflows.

### 3. Strategic Vision: Orbital AI Data Centers (SpaceX & xAI Synergy)
Following SpaceX’s acquisition of xAI, the prospectus outlines a highly ambitious, vertically integrated vision to move AI compute infrastructure off-planet to bypass terrestrial power and cooling constraints:
*   **The Synergy:** The merger unites SpaceX’s launch capabilities, reusable rockets, and scaled satellite manufacturing with xAI’s frontier AI development and Starlink's global connectivity network.
*   **Massive Orbital Constellations:** The company envisions the cost-effective and rapid deployment of massive AI compute satellite constellations in **Sun-synchronous orbit**, potentially consisting of **millions of satellites**.
*   **Handling Energy-Intensive Workloads:** These orbital data centers are specifically designed to handle energy-intensive AI workloads (such as massive inference demand) at a "far greater scale and efficiency than terrestrial alternatives."
*   **Global Low-Latency Delivery:** Starlink's network will provide the low-latency, global connectivity required to beam the outputs of these space-based AI compute clusters back to users and enterprises anywhere on Earth.

Based on the provided prospectus preamble excerpts, here are the definitions, caveats, and limitations for Monthly Active Users (MAUs), daily posts, and broader user engagement metrics across the X and Grok platforms.

### 1. Monthly Active Users (MAUs)
**Definition:** 
MAU is defined as the total number of registered users who have interacted with Grok or X through web browsers or mobile applications at least once during the 30-day period ending on the date of measurement. 

**Methodology & Inclusions:**
*   **Registration Requirement:** Only users who have formally registered for an X or Grok account are included in this metric.
*   **Deduplication:** When presenting *combined* MAUs across both platforms, the company attempts to identify and account for users who access both Grok and X based on sign-in traffic to prevent double-counting.
*   **Feature Tracking:** The company tracks the specific subset of MAUs who utilize Grok’s AI features versus those who do not, based on the source of their server requests.

**Caveats & Limitations:**
*   **Estimation & Duplication:** The methodology only provides a "reasonable approximation" of unique users. It may not fully capture all instances of cross-platform duplication, meaning reported MAUs should be viewed strictly as an *estimate*.
*   **Strategic Deprioritization:** The company explicitly states that it manages the business to drive sustainable revenue growth, operating margin, and profitability. Therefore, growing or maintaining MAU levels is *not* a primary business objective.
*   **Market Fluctuations:** In the highly competitive consumer AI market, usage metrics and app downloads for Grok can fluctuate significantly (including periods of decline) due to competitor model releases, product update cycles, and shifts in user behavior.

### 2. Daily Posts
**Definition:** 
"Daily posts" refers to the aggregate daily volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes, and media shared by users on the X platform, combined with the real-time interactions, analysis, and generative capabilities provided to a user by Grok. 

**Inclusions:**
*   **AI-Generated Content:** The metric explicitly includes posts and content generated by AI or accounts managed by AI.
*   **Volume:** The text notes this real-time information stream consists of approximately 350 million daily posts.

**Caveats & Limitations:**
*   **Content Limitations for AI Training:** While this massive dataset is used to ground Grok’s "truth-seeking" capabilities in up-to-date knowledge and diverse viewpoints, the company notes that its use of the X dataset is "subject to some limitations for certain content."
*   **Quality Dependency:** The value of this metric is heavily dependent on user-generated content. If users stop contributing, or if the platform is flooded with problematic/offensive content, the utility of this data stream for AI development and advertiser engagement degrades.

### 3. User Engagement Metrics (Broadly)
**Definition & Tracking:**
*   **Supported Accounts:** The company tracks "supported accounts active in the last twelve months" (noted as over 1.3 billion) as a broader measure of the ecosystem's total reachable scale.
*   **Monetizable Engagement:** Engagement is tracked to facilitate high-impact advertising inventory, performance-based marketing tools, and unified user experiences across communication, content discovery, commerce, and financial services.

**Caveats, Risks & Limitations:**
*   **Platform Migration Risk:** Engagement metrics are highly vulnerable to user migration. Losing users to competing platforms directly negatively impacts potential subscription and advertising revenues.
*   **The "Content-Engagement-AI" Flywheel Risk:** There is a critical downstream limitation tied to engagement. If user engagement declines, or if content quality drops (e.g., offensive material), it not only harms X's brand reputation and monetizable usage but also *starves the ongoing development of the Grok AI product*, which relies on X's real-time data and user-generated content to maintain its competitive differentiator.
*   **Intense Competition for Attention:** The company warns that the consumer AI and social media markets are characterized by an "intense competition for user attention," making long-term engagement stability inherently uncertain and subject to rapid shifts in consumer preferences. 

### Summary
The prospectus makes it clear that while **MAUs** and **Daily Posts** are massive in scale (550M MAUs and 350M daily posts), they are treated as *inputs* for revenue and AI training rather than vanity metrics to be grown at all costs. The primary limitations revolve around the technical difficulties of perfectly deduplicating cross-platform users, the inclusion of AI-generated noise in post volumes, and the severe downstream business risks if user engagement or content quality on X were to decline.

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve more specific information about the Grok distribution advantage, the specific caveats around the 117M figure, and what limitations the filing places on interpreting these metrics.

arguments.prompt: What does the filing say about Grok penetration rate, conversion rate, or percentage of X users who use Grok features in {{spacex_s1}}?
What are the risk factors related to X platform engagement decline, user migration, content quality degradation, and Grok dependency on X data in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided S-1 filing excerpts, here is what the document says regarding Grok's penetration rate, conversion rate, and the percentage of users utilizing Grok features:

### 1. Grok Penetration Rate
The filing does not provide a specific numerical percentage for the penetration rate, but it discusses it as a key strategic focus and current area of opportunity:
* **Strategic Goal:** The company plans to "continue adding new features and functionality while releasing increasingly capable Grok models to increase the penetration rate of our subscriber base."
* **Current Status:** The filing explicitly notes that the company is still in the early stages of monetizing the user base, stating: *"While our subscriber growth has been strong, we believe we are still early in increasing paid penetration across our Grok user base."*

### 2. Conversion Rate
Similar to the penetration rate, the filing does not disclose a specific mathematical **conversion rate** (e.g., a percentage of free users who convert to paid). Instead, it outlines the strategy and provides absolute subscriber metrics:
* **Strategic Focus:** The company is focused on "converting a greater portion of our user base into paying subscribers" and specifically lists "increasing X Premium subscriber conversion" as a core method to drive X revenue growth. 
* **Absolute Subscriber Metrics:** As of March 31, 2026, the company reached approximately **6.3 million active paid subscribers**. This is broken down into:
  * **~4.4 million** X Premium and Premium+ paid subscribers.
  * **~1.9 million** SuperGrok, SuperGrok Heavy, and SuperGrok Lite paid subscribers.

### 3. Percentage of Users Who Use Grok Features
While the filing does not explicitly state a "percentage," it provides the exact raw user metrics required to calculate it. 
* **The Raw Numbers:** As of March 31, 2026, the combined, deduplicated Monthly Active Users (MAUs) across Grok and X was **~550 million**. Out of that total, **~117 million MAUs** used Grok’s AI features.
* **The Implied Percentage:** This means that approximately **21.3%** (117 million / 550 million) of the combined platform's monthly active users engaged with Grok's AI features.
* **Tracking Methodology:** The filing notes that they track this specific subset of users "based on the source of their server requests" to differentiate between those who use Grok's AI features and those who do not. 
* *Note on the metric:* The 550 million MAU figure represents the *combined* unique users across both X and Grok (accounting for overlap so users aren't double-counted), rather than the X platform entirely in isolation.

Based on the provided S-1 Risk Factors and Business Overview excerpts, the risks surrounding the X platform's engagement, user migration, content quality, and Grok's reliance on X data are deeply interconnected. A decline in any one of these areas creates a cascading effect that threatens the company's AI development, advertising revenue, and new product rollouts. 

Here is a detailed breakdown of these risk factors as outlined in the filing:

### 1. X Platform Engagement Decline & User Migration
*   **User Migration & Revenue Loss:** The filing explicitly warns that losing users who migrate to competing platforms will negatively impact potential subscription and advertising revenues. Advertisers generally lack long-term commitments to X and may quickly reduce or discontinue spending if engagement drops.
*   **Drivers of Disengagement:** Engagement may decline if users stop contributing content, if the platform fails to provide valuable and timely information, or if the platform becomes saturated with problematic or offensive content. 
*   **Strategic Prioritization of Revenue over MAUs:** The company notes that while it has over 1.3 billion supported accounts and ~550 million MAUs, it manages the business with the objective of driving "sustainable revenue growth and profitability rather than with the primary objective of growing or maintaining MAU levels." This strategic stance implies a tolerance for potential user base stagnation or shrinkage in pursuit of margins, which could inherently reduce the volume of user-generated data.
*   **Impact on New Initiatives (The "Money Product"):** A declining or disengaged user base poses a severe threat to the planned public launch of the "Money Product" (financial services on X). The filing notes that X will be competing against large, established financial and tech companies with significantly greater resources; without a highly engaged user base, establishing a meaningful market position will be difficult.

### 2. Content Quality Degradation & AI Safety Risks
*   **Proliferation of Offensive Content:** If content considered "problematic or offensive" becomes prevalent, it drives away users, harms the brand's reputation, and triggers advertiser boycotts.
*   **Risks of "Less Constrained" AI Modes:** Grok offers features designed to be candid or irreverent, such as the **“Spicy” Imagine Mode** and **“Unhinged” Voice Mode**. The S-1 warns that these modes present heightened risks of generating explicit content, misinformation, deepfakes, nonconsensual imagery, and discriminatory outputs. 
*   **Severe Legal and Regulatory Backlash:** Content quality issues have already materialized into major legal liabilities. The company is facing active investigations and putative class-action lawsuits regarding allegations that its AI products were used to create nonconsensual explicit images, including content representing children in sexualized contexts. 
*   **App Store Delisting Threats:** Failure to moderate content in compliance with third-party developer agreements (Apple App Store and Google Play Store) could result in the suspension or removal of the X and Grok mobile apps, materially limiting user access and engagement.

### 3. Grok’s Dependency on X Data (The "Data Flywheel" Vulnerability)
*   **The Core Differentiator:** Grok’s primary competitive advantage is its native integration with X. The filing states that X provides "uniquely dynamic data for model training and real-time context integration," which differentiates Grok from other AI models. Public X data is described as foundational to Grok's training and reasoning capabilities.
*   **Downstream Impact of Data Degradation:** The S-1 explicitly links X's health to Grok's viability: *"A decline in users on the X platform, or the volume or quality of their content... could also impact the ongoing development of our AI product."* If user migration or disengagement reduces the volume of high-quality public discourse, Grok's training data pipeline will starve or degrade.
*   **Susceptibility to "Data Poisoning":** The filing admits that Grok and other AI products have been alleged to be susceptible to "data poisoning" in the past. If X's content moderation fails and the platform is flooded with spam, bot-generated text, or malicious data, Grok's models will ingest this toxic data, leading to flawed, biased, or "hallucinatory" outputs.
*   **Regulatory Restrictions on Data Usage:** Evolving data protection laws threaten this data dependency. For example, the **Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC)** launched a large-scale inquiry in February 2026 into whether Grok's processing of EU users' personal data (including children's data) complies with the GDPR. Regulatory actions could legally restrict Grok's ability to train on X's user-generated data in major jurisdictions.

### 4. Compounding Market & Competitive Risks
*   **Extreme Market Volatility:** The consumer AI market is characterized by rapid model iteration and intense competition. The filing warns that Grok's download and usage metrics can "fluctuate significantly (including periods of decreased Grok app downloads and user activity)" based on competitor releases and shifting user trends.
*   **Third-Party Misuse:** The company lacks total control over how third parties use its AI APIs and tools. Malicious actors using Grok for spear-phishing, social engineering, or generating illegal content further degrades the perceived quality and safety of the ecosystem, inviting scrutiny from bodies like the **Federal Trade Commission (FTC)**, which is actively investigating the safety of chatbots acting as companions to children and teens.

### Summary
The S-1 outlines a highly fragile ecosystem where **X's social platform health** and **Grok's AI capabilities** are entirely codependent. If X suffers from user migration or a degradation in content quality (due to toxic user behavior or controversial "unhinged" AI features), it directly poisons the data well required to train Grok. Conversely, if regulatory bodies (like the FTC or EU DPC) or App Stores restrict Grok's data access or distribution due to safety concerns, it diminishes X's value proposition as an "everything app," accelerating user decline and jeopardizing future monetization efforts like the Money Product.

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me get more specific information about the metric caveats - particularly around what the 117M figure does and doesn't tell us, and what the filing says about the limitations of these metrics.

arguments.prompt: What does the filing say about how Grok AI features are defined, what counts as using Grok features, and the methodology for tracking Grok usage versus X-only usage in {{spacex_s1}}?
What does the filing say about daily posts including AI-generated content, bot content, and the composition or quality of the 350 million daily posts figure in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided sections of the filing, here is how Grok AI features, usage, and tracking methodologies are defined:

### 1. How Grok AI Features are Defined
The filing defines "Grok" as the company's family of frontier, "truth-seeking" AI models. The specific consumer and enterprise features powered by Grok include:
*   **Grok Chat:** The primary conversational interface for text or voice queries, reasoning, coding, web search, and multimodal analysis.
*   **Grok Imagine:** The generative visual and multimedia suite used for producing high-quality images and short videos.
*   **Grok Voice:** A real-time, multilingual speech engine.
*   **Alternative Output Modes:** Less constrained or irreverent features designed for candid outputs, specifically named as **"Spicy" Imagine Mode** and **"Unhinged" Voice Mode**.
*   **Enterprise & Developer Tools:** Grok API, Grok Business, and Grok Enterprise for integrating models into external software and internal business workflows.

### 2. What Counts as "Using" Grok Features
A user is counted as having used Grok's AI features if their interactions route to the specific backend infrastructure powering the AI. The filing explicitly states that the company determines whether a user has utilized Grok's AI features **"based on the source of their server requests."** 

### 3. Methodology for Tracking Grok Usage vs. X-Only Usage
To calculate Monthly Active Users (MAUs) and differentiate between X-only users, Grok users, and the combined user base, the company uses the following methodology:
*   **Differentiating via Server Requests:** The company tracks the specific subset of users who used Grok’s AI features versus those who only used X by analyzing the **source of their server requests**.
*   **De-duplication via Sign-In Traffic:** To calculate a *combined* MAU across both platforms without double-counting people who use both X and Grok, the company identifies and accounts for overlapping users based on **sign-in traffic**.
*   **Registration Requirement:** Only users who have officially registered for an X or Grok account are included in the metrics.
*   **30-Day Activity Window:** A user is considered "active" if they interact with Grok or X via web browsers or mobile apps at least once during the 30-day period ending on the measurement date.
*   **Estimation Caveat:** The filing notes that while this methodology provides a "reasonable approximation" of unique users, it "may not fully capture all instances of duplication." Therefore, the reported combined MAU should be viewed as an estimate.

Based on the provided filing excerpts, here is what the document says regarding daily posts, the inclusion of AI/bot content, and the composition and quality of the 350 million daily posts figure:

### 1. Inclusion of AI-Generated and Bot Content
The filing explicitly acknowledges that its user and engagement metrics are inflated by or inclusive of automated and AI-generated activity:
*   **Definition of "Daily Posts":** The glossary defines daily posts as the aggregate volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes, and media shared on X, as well as real-time interactions and generative capabilities provided by Grok. Crucially, it states that this metric **"may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI."**
*   **Definition of "Supported Accounts":** The metric for accounts logging into X or Grok includes "a human, bot or similar account." The filing admits that the total number of supported accounts **"may include fake, spam or bot accounts if they are active."**

### 2. The Strategic Value of the 350 Million Figure
The "approximately 350 million daily posts" figure is heavily touted as a core competitive differentiator and a strategic asset for the company's AI segment:
*   **Real-Time Information Stream:** It is described as a "massive dataset" and a "proprietary access to a real-time information stream" that gives Grok an advantage in "freshness, relevance, and contextual awareness."
*   **Grounding AI Outputs:** The filing claims this direct access to human discourse and real-time data enhances Grok’s "truth-seeking capabilities" by grounding its outputs in up-to-date knowledge and diverse viewpoints, allowing it to analyze real-time global events. 

### 3. Caveats on Composition, Quality, and Limitations
Despite touting the sheer volume of the 350 million daily posts, the filing's Risk Factors and definitions reveal significant caveats regarding the actual quality and usability of this data composition:
*   **Content Limitations:** The filing notes that while the dataset is massive, its use for AI training is **"subject to some limitations for certain content."**
*   **Vulnerability to Quality Declines:** The company warns that a decline in the **"volume or quality of their content"** could directly and negatively "impact the ongoing development of our AI product," which relies heavily on this user-generated data. 
*   **Problematic and Offensive Content:** The filing acknowledges the risk that "content that is considered to be problematic or offensive" may be made available on the platform. Because Grok ingests this real-time stream, the AI is exposed to the platform's content moderation struggles.
*   **Toxicity and Misinformation Risks:** The risk factors highlight that the platform's content composition exposes the company to risks related to "harmful, misleading or illegal content, accuracy, misinformation and deepfakes, bias, discrimination, toxicity, sycophancy, [and] AI deception." 

In summary, while the 350 million daily posts figure is marketed as a massive, real-time "truth-seeking" dataset that gives Grok a unique competitive edge, the filing legally discloses that this stream is composed of a mix of humans, bots, spam, and AI-generated posts, and is highly vulnerable to quality degradation, toxicity, and content limitations that could hinder AI development.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me also check if there's anything about whether the 117M figure represents "deep" usage vs. casual/tangential usage, and whether the filing distinguishes between different types of Grok feature usage.

arguments.prompt: What does the filing say about Grok usage depth, frequency of Grok usage, retention, engagement intensity, or whether Grok users are casual versus heavy users in {{spacex_s1}}?
What does the filing say about X standalone MAUs separate from Grok, or the total X platform user base without Grok in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided sections of the S-1 filing's preamble, the document does not provide granular, internal data metrics (like exact retention cohorts or daily session lengths) regarding Grok's usage depth or retention. Instead, it frames user behavior, engagement intensity, and user segmentation through the lens of **product strategy, subscription tiering, and targeted use cases**. 

Here is what the filing explicitly says regarding your specific areas of interest:

### 1. Casual vs. Heavy Users (User Segmentation)
The filing clearly distinguishes between casual/basic users and heavy users, primarily through its **subscription tier strategy**:
*   **Power Users (Heavy Users):** The document explicitly uses the term **"power users."** It states that higher pricing tiers (such as *SuperGrok* and *SuperGrok Heavy*) unlock expanded access to advanced models, increased usage limits, priority compute, and premium features specifically **"tailored to power users and enterprise-grade applications."**
*   **Casual/Basic Users:** The company offers tiers like *SuperGrok Lite* and basic access for users who only need foundational capabilities. 
*   **Monetization & Penetration:** The filing notes that while Grok has roughly 117 million MAUs using its AI features, the company is **"still early in increasing paid penetration across our Grok user base."** This implies that the vast majority of current Grok users are likely casual or free-tier users, and the company's strategic goal is to convert them into paying "power users."

### 2. Frequency of Grok Usage & Engagement Intensity
Rather than disclosing raw frequency metrics (e.g., "users open the app X times a day"), the filing defines Grok's engagement intensity by the **nature of the workflows** it targets:
*   **High-Frequency Use Cases:** The filing states that Grok's real-time access to X's data allows it to **"best serve high-frequency, high-value use cases across consumer and enterprise AI applications."**
*   **Developer/Coding Workflows (Cursor Partnership):** When discussing the strategic partnership with Cursor, the filing highlights the immense value of **"high-frequency coding workflows."** It notes that AI-assisted coding generates rapid feedback cycles and frequent, mission-critical usage, which provides deep, context-rich interaction data to train Grok.
*   **Real-Time Interactions:** Engagement intensity is also driven by Grok's integration into X, which generates "real-time interactions, analysis and generative capabilities" tied to the 350 million daily posts on the platform.

### 3. Usage Depth
The filing describes usage depth in terms of **integration and capability** rather than time-spent metrics:
*   **Deep Platform Integration:** Grok’s "deep integration" with X is cited as a primary competitive differentiator, allowing the AI to ground its outputs in up-to-date knowledge, human discourse, and diverse viewpoints.
*   **Multimodal & Agentic Depth:** The depth of the product itself is expanding beyond basic text chat into complex reasoning, code execution, multimodal analysis (Grok Imagine for image/video), and real-time speech (Grok Voice), which naturally demands deeper, more complex user engagement.

### 4. Retention
**The filing does not explicitly discuss Grok's user retention rates or churn.** 
*   The only mention of the word **"retention"** in the provided text is tied to **X's advertising ecosystem**, not Grok's consumer AI retention. It states that X’s real-time content stream and engagement feedback are expected to strengthen advertising product performance, improve outcomes for advertisers, and increase *advertiser/campaign retention*. 
*   However, the filing does warn in the Risk Factors that **"download and other usage metrics... and user activity can fluctuate significantly"** due to competitor model releases and shifting user behavior, acknowledging that maintaining user retention in the consumer AI market is highly volatile and competitive.

### Summary
The preamble portrays Grok's user base as a massive, broad funnel (117 million MAUs) consisting largely of casual or free users, with a strategic imperative to convert them into **"power users"** via tiered subscriptions (*SuperGrok Heavy*). The company views Grok's engagement intensity not through casual chat, but through its ability to capture **"high-frequency, high-value"** workflows (like coding and real-time information synthesis) that deeply integrate into users' daily digital and professional routines.

Based on the provided filing excerpts, the document **does not explicitly report a standalone MAU figure for X separate from Grok**, nor does it define a "total X platform user base without Grok" as an isolated metric. Instead, the company reports a **combined, deduplicated MAU** for both platforms and provides a subset metric for Grok usage. 

Here is exactly what the filing says regarding X and Grok user metrics, tracking methodologies, and scale:

### 1. Combined MAUs and the Grok Subset
*   **Total Combined MAUs:** The integrated platforms across Grok and X have approximately **550 million MAUs** (as of March 31, 2026).
*   **Grok AI Users:** Out of the total MAUs, approximately **117 million MAUs** specifically used Grok’s AI features. 
*   **Supported Accounts:** The platforms have over **1.3 billion supported accounts** active in the last twelve months.
*   *Implied X-Only/Non-Grok Users:* While not explicitly stated as a standalone number, the users who engage with X but *do not* use Grok's AI features would conceptually make up the difference between the 550 million combined MAUs and the 117 million Grok MAUs. 

### 2. Methodology for Tracking and Deduplication
*   **Avoiding Double-Counting:** The filing states, *"In presenting combined MAUs across the two platforms, we seek to identify and account for users who access both Grok and X based on sign-in traffic so that such users are not double-counted when measuring MAU."*
*   **Tracking Non-Grok Users:** The company explicitly notes that it tracks the divide between Grok and non-Grok users internally: *"We track the subset of users who used Grok’s AI features and those who have not based on the source of their server requests."*
*   **Estimations:** The company caveats that their methodology *"may not fully capture all instances of duplication,"* meaning the 550 million figure is an estimate of unique users across both platforms.

### 3. Other X-Specific Scale & Engagement Metrics
While a standalone X MAU number is omitted, the filing highlights X's specific scale and engagement through other metrics:
*   **Advertising Scale:** When discussing the advertising business, the filing refers to driving traffic and conversions *"among X’s **hundreds of millions of MAUs**."*
*   **Daily Content Generation:** X's global user base generates approximately **350 million daily posts**, which serves as a foundational data engine for Grok's real-time training.
*   **X Paid Subscribers:** As of March 31, 2026, there were approximately **4.4 million X Premium and Premium+ paid subscribers** (which is tracked separately from the ~1.9 million SuperGrok paid subscribers). 

**Summary:** 
The filing intentionally bridges X and Grok into a single "integrated AI platform" metric (550 million MAUs) to highlight the unified ecosystem. It isolates the **117 million** users who actively use Grok's AI features, but relies on the broader **"hundreds of millions"** phrasing and **350 million daily posts** to describe the underlying X platform's base that may not be actively engaging with Grok.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have a very comprehensive picture. Let me synthesize what an analyst CAN and CANNOT conclude about Grok's distribution advantage through X, based on the specific figures (117M of 550M MAUs, 350M daily posts) and the filing's metric caveats.

Let me organize my analysis:

**What an analyst CAN conclude:**

1. **Grok has meaningful but minority penetration of the X ecosystem**: 117M / 550M = ~21.3% of combined MAUs used Grok AI features. This shows Grok has reached a non-trivial portion of the user base but the majority (~78.7%) have NOT used Grok features.

2. **X provides a massive real-time data moat**: 350M daily posts represent a significant real-time data stream that competitors like OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic don't have direct access to.

3. **The distribution channel is native and integrated**: Grok is embedded directly in the X interface (prominent icon), meaning users don't need to download a separate app or visit a separate website.

4. **Paid conversion is very low**: Only ~1.9M SuperGrok subscribers out of 117M Grok MAUs = ~1.6% paid penetration, and the filing explicitly states they are "still early in increasing paid penetration."

5. **The filing itself acknowledges the metrics are estimates**: Deduplication methodology "may not fully capture all instances of duplication."

6. **The company prioritizes revenue/profitability over MAU growth**: This is explicitly stated, meaning MAU figures are not a managed KPI.

**What an analyst CANNOT conclude:**

1. **Cannot conclude the 117M represents "deep" or "meaningful" Grok usage**: The metric counts anyone whose server requests routed to Grok infrastructure at least once in 30 days. A single casual interaction (e.g., clicking on a Grok-suggested summary) counts the same as a power user.

2. **Cannot isolate X's standalone MAU**: The 550M is a combined, deduplicated figure. We don't know how many are X-only, Grok-only, or both. The denominator for calculating Grok's true "penetration of X" is unknown.

3. **Cannot conclude the 350M daily posts are all high-quality human-generated content**: The filing explicitly states daily posts "may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI" and supported accounts "may include fake, spam or bot accounts."

4. **Cannot conclude the distribution advantage is durable or defensible**: The filing warns that usage metrics "can fluctuate significantly" due to competitor releases, and user migration to competing platforms is a material risk.

5. **Cannot conclude Grok's data advantage translates to model superiority**: The filing notes content is "subject to some limitations for certain content" and acknowledges risks of "data poisoning."

6. **Cannot determine retention or engagement frequency**: No retention rates, session frequency, or time-spent metrics are disclosed for Grok users.

7. **Cannot conclude the 21.3% penetration rate is growing or stable**: No historical trend for the Grok MAU subset is provided (only the combined 550M is noted as up from 520M in December 2025).

8. **Cannot assess the quality of the "distribution"**: Having Grok embedded in X doesn't tell us whether users discover Grok organically, are nudged via UI prompts, or accidentally trigger Grok features.

Let me now write a comprehensive answer.
Now I have all the data I need. Let me compose a comprehensive, well-structured answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Grok's Distribution Advantage Through X: What the Filing's Metrics Do and Do Not Support

### The Key Figures (as of March 31, 2026)

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Combined deduplicated MAUs (X + Grok) | ~550 million |
| MAUs who used Grok AI features | ~117 million |
| Implied Grok penetration of combined MAUs | ~21.3% |
| Implied non-Grok users | ~433 million (~78.7%) |
| Daily posts on X (incl. AI-generated) | ~350 million |
| Paid SuperGrok subscribers | ~1.9 million |
| Paid SuperGrok penetration of Grok MAUs | ~1.6% |

*All figures reflect the recast consolidated entity (including xAI and X Holdings).*

---

### What an Analyst CAN Conclude

**1. Grok has achieved meaningful but distinctly minority reach within the X ecosystem.**
At ~21.3%, roughly one in five combined-platform MAUs triggered a Grok AI feature at least once in the trailing 30-day window. This confirms that Grok is not a niche product—it has reached over 100 million users—but the vast majority (~78.7%) of the platform's active users have *not* engaged with Grok.

**2. X provides a real-time data asset that no standalone AI lab can replicate.**
The ~350 million daily posts constitute a proprietary, continuously refreshed corpus of global discourse. Competitors like OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic rely on periodic web crawls or licensed datasets; Grok has a native, real-time ingestion pipeline. The filing describes this as providing "freshness, relevance, and contextual awareness" that grounds Grok's outputs.

**3. The distribution is natively integrated, not bolted on.**
Grok is embedded directly in the X interface via a prominent icon, enabling conversational AI, Grok-assisted post creation, enhanced search, and trending analysis without requiring users to leave the app or download a separate product. This is a structural distribution advantage over standalone AI chatbots that require separate user acquisition.

**4. Monetization of the Grok user base is nascent.**
Only ~1.9 million SuperGrok paid subscribers exist against ~117 million Grok MAUs—a ~1.6% paid conversion rate. The filing explicitly states the company is "still early in increasing paid penetration across our Grok user base." This means the distribution advantage has not yet been translated into meaningful direct AI subscription revenue at scale.

**5. The company does not manage for MAU growth.**
The filing explicitly states that the business is managed to drive "sustainable revenue growth, operating margin, and profitability" and that "growing or maintaining MAU levels is *not* a primary business objective." This means MAU figures are not an optimized KPI and could stagnate or decline by management design.

**6. The metrics are acknowledged estimates, not precise counts.**
The filing states the deduplication methodology provides only a "reasonable approximation" and "may not fully capture all instances of duplication." Both the 550M and 117M figures should be treated as estimates with acknowledged measurement error.

---

### What an Analyst CANNOT Conclude

**1. Cannot conclude the 117M represents deep, habitual, or meaningful Grok usage.**
The filing defines a Grok MAU as any registered user whose server requests routed to Grok infrastructure at least *once* in a 30-day period. A user who accidentally clicked a Grok-suggested summary, received a single Grok-generated search result, or tried the feature once and never returned is counted identically to a daily power user. No frequency, session depth, retention, or time-spent metrics are disclosed.

**2. Cannot isolate X's standalone MAU to calculate true Grok penetration of X.**
The 550M figure is a *combined, deduplicated* metric across both X and Grok. The filing does not disclose a standalone X-only MAU. Some of the 550M may be Grok-only users who never use X's social features. Without knowing X's standalone MAU, an analyst cannot determine what percentage of X's social-media user base has adopted Grok—the true measure of "distribution advantage."

**3. Cannot conclude the 350M daily posts represent high-quality, human-generated training data.**
The filing explicitly discloses that daily posts **"may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI."** Separately, supported accounts **"may include fake, spam, or bot accounts if they are active."** The composition breakdown (human vs. bot vs. AI-generated) is not disclosed. An analyst cannot determine what portion of the 350M daily posts constitutes genuine human discourse useful for AI training versus automated noise.

**4. Cannot conclude the distribution advantage is durable or defensible.**
The filing warns that Grok's "download and other usage metrics... and user activity can fluctuate significantly (including periods of decreased Grok app downloads and user activity)" based on competitor model releases and shifting user behavior. The distribution advantage is contingent on X's platform health, which the filing identifies as a material risk factor.

**5. Cannot conclude Grok's data access translates to model superiority.**
The filing acknowledges that use of X's dataset is "subject to some limitations for certain content" and discloses risks of "data poisoning"—where spam, bot-generated text, or malicious data ingested from X could lead to "flawed, biased, or hallucinatory outputs." Having more data does not automatically mean having better models, and the filing does not claim model performance superiority.

**6. Cannot assess Grok user retention or engagement trajectory.**
No retention rates, cohort analyses, churn figures, or historical trend data for the 117M Grok MAU subset are disclosed. The only historical comparison provided is that combined MAUs grew from ~520M (December 2025) to ~550M (March 2026), but the Grok-specific subset trend is unknown. An analyst cannot determine whether Grok adoption is accelerating, plateauing, or declining.

**7. Cannot determine whether Grok usage is organic or UI-driven.**
The filing describes Grok's "prominent icon" and native integration but does not disclose what percentage of Grok interactions are user-initiated versus prompted by UI nudges, default settings, or Grok-generated content injected into the X feed. The "distribution advantage" may partly reflect interface design rather than genuine user demand.

**8. Cannot conclude the data flywheel is self-reinforcing without degradation risk.**
The filing explicitly links X's health to Grok's viability: "A decline in users on the X platform, or the volume or quality of their content... could also impact the ongoing development of our AI product." The flywheel is bidirectional—content quality degradation, regulatory restrictions (e.g., the Irish DPC's GDPR inquiry launched February 2026), or app-store delisting could impair both the data supply and the distribution channel simultaneously.

---

### Summary Assessment

The filing supports the conclusion that X provides Grok with a **structurally unique distribution channel and real-time data asset** that no standalone AI competitor possesses. However, the filing's own metric definitions and caveats prevent an analyst from concluding that this distribution advantage is **deep** (usage intensity unknown), **durable** (retention and trajectory unknown), **high-quality** (bot/AI content contamination unknown), or **effectively monetized** (1.6% paid conversion). The 21.3% penetration figure is a ceiling on reach, not a floor on engagement—and the ~78.7% of combined MAUs who have *not* used Grok represent either a large growth opportunity or evidence that native distribution alone does not drive AI adoption.

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}
